questions.question:
Two popular routing algorithms are Distance Vector (DV) and Link State (LS) routing. Which of the following are true?
(S1): Count to infinity is a problem only with DV and not LS routing
(S2): In LS, the shortest path algorithm is run only at one node
(S3): In DV, the shortest path algorithm is run only at one node
(S4): LS requires lesser number of network messages than DV

A. S1, S2 and S4 only
B. S1, S3 and S4 only
C. S2 and S3 only
D. S1 and S4 only
questions.to_find: Which of the given statements about DV and LS routing algorithms are true

understand.plain: Distance Vector (DV) routing uses Bellman-Ford where each router shares its routing table with neighbors. Link State (LS) routing uses Dijkstra where each router broadcasts link-state info to all routers, and each router independently runs Dijkstra. This question asks which statements about these two protocols are correct.

keywords.explain: A problem in DV routing where incorrect routing information slowly propagates through the network, causing route metrics to increment indefinitely before converging.
keywords.example: If A-B link fails, A and B may keep incrementing the hop count to infinity before realizing the route is broken.

keywords.explain: Each router floods its local link-state information to all routers in the network; then every router independently runs Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm on the complete topology.
keywords.example: OSPF is a link-state protocol. After receiving all LSAs, each router runs Dijkstra locally.

keywords.explain: Each router maintains a table of distances to all destinations and shares this table with its immediate neighbors. Uses distributed Bellman-Ford algorithm.
keywords.example: RIP is a DV protocol. Router A tells neighbor B: I can reach X in 3 hops.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='235' height='185' rx='8' class='bg'/><rect x='255' y='5' width='255' height='185' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='122' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Distance Vector (DV)</text><text x='382' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Link State (LS)</text><text x='15' y='60' class='sub ink'>- Bellman-Ford (distributed)</text><text x='15' y='82' class='sub ink'>- Share tables with neighbors</text><text x='15' y='104' class='sub ink'>- Count-to-infinity problem YES</text><text x='15' y='126' class='sub ink'>- All nodes run algorithm</text><text x='15' y='148' class='sub ink'>- Periodic updates (more msgs)</text><text x='265' y='60' class='sub ink'>- Dijkstra (each node)</text><text x='265' y='82' class='sub ink'>- Flood link-state to all</text><text x='265' y='104' class='sub ink'>- Count-to-infinity NO</text><text x='265' y='126' class='sub ink'>- All nodes run Dijkstra</text><text x='265' y='148' class='sub ink'>- Event-triggered (fewer msgs)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Comparison table: DV uses distributed Bellman-Ford with neighbor table sharing and periodic updates (more messages); LS uses Dijkstra at every node with link-state flooding and event-triggered updates (fewer messages in steady state).

given.aim: Identify which statements about DV and LS routing are correct

terms.term: S1: Count to infinity only in DV
terms.meaning: Count-to-infinity is a well-known instability problem unique to Distance Vector protocols caused by routing loops during convergence.
terms.example: RIP (DV) suffers count-to-infinity; OSPF (LS) does not.

terms.term: S2: LS shortest path run at only one node
terms.meaning: In LS routing every router receives the complete topology and independently runs Dijkstra — not just one node.
terms.example: In OSPF all routers run SPF independently.

terms.term: S4: LS requires fewer messages than DV
terms.meaning: DV sends periodic full routing table updates to all neighbors. LS sends updates only when topology changes (event-triggered), resulting in fewer messages in steady state.
terms.example: RIP sends updates every 30 seconds; OSPF only on link change.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement S1-S4 against known properties of DV (Bellman-Ford, neighbor table sharing, periodic updates, count-to-infinity) and LS (Dijkstra at each node, link-state flooding, event-triggered updates). Pick the option that lists only the true statements.

steps.title: Evaluate S1 and S2
steps.apply:
S1: Count-to-infinity is caused by routing loops in DV due to distributed Bellman-Ford convergence. LS has complete topology so no such loops form. S1 is TRUE.
S2: In LS routing, Dijkstra is run independently at EVERY node (each has full topology map), not just one node. S2 is FALSE.
steps.note: Count-to-infinity is the classic weakness of DV protocols like RIP.

steps.title: Evaluate S3 and S4
steps.apply:
S3: In DV routing, every node runs the distributed Bellman-Ford algorithm to update its own routing table — not just one node. S3 is FALSE.
S4: DV sends periodic updates (e.g., every 30 s in RIP) to all neighbors regardless of topology changes. LS sends updates only when a link-state change occurs (event-triggered). Therefore LS requires fewer network messages in steady state. S4 is TRUE.
steps.note: Although LS initially floods LSPs to all nodes, in steady state it generates fewer messages than periodic DV updates.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: True statements: S1 and S4. False: S2 and S3. This matches option D: S1 and S4 only.

questions.formula_note: Properties of Distance Vector (Bellman-Ford, periodic updates, count-to-infinity) vs Link State (Dijkstra at every node, event-triggered flooding, no count-to-infinity).

questions.question:
Consider the routing protocols given in List I and the names given in List II:

List I:
(i) Distance Vector routing
(ii) Link state routing

List II:
(a) Bellman-Ford
(b) Dijkstra

For matching of items in List I with those in List II, which ONE of the following options is CORRECT?

A. (i) - (a) and (ii) - (b)
B. (i) - (a) and (ii) - (a)
C. (i) - (b) and (ii) - (a)
D. (i) - (b) and (ii) - (b)
questions.to_find: Match Distance Vector routing and Link State routing to the correct underlying algorithms (Bellman-Ford or Dijkstra)

understand.plain: This question tests whether you know which shortest-path algorithm underlies each routing protocol. Distance Vector routing is based on the Bellman-Ford algorithm, while Link State routing is based on Dijkstra's algorithm. You need to match them correctly.

keywords.explain: A dynamic programming based shortest-path algorithm that works by relaxing edges iteratively. It can handle negative weights and works in a distributed fashion — ideal for DV routing.
keywords.example: RIP uses Bellman-Ford: each router updates its table based on neighbor information over multiple rounds.

keywords.explain: A greedy shortest-path algorithm that picks the nearest unvisited node at each step. Requires complete graph knowledge — used in LS routing where every node has the full topology.
keywords.example: OSPF uses Dijkstra: each router runs SPF on its link-state database to compute shortest paths.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='10' width='220' height='140' rx='8' class='bg'/><rect x='255' y='10' width='220' height='140' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='115' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Distance Vector</text><text x='365' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Link State</text><text x='115' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(i)</text><text x='365' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(ii)</text><text x='115' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>(a)</text><text x='365' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>(b)</text><text x='115' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Bellman-Ford</text><text x='365' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Dijkstra</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two columns: Distance Vector (i) maps to Bellman-Ford (a); Link State (ii) maps to Dijkstra (b).

given.aim: Correctly match Distance Vector routing to Bellman-Ford and Link State routing to Dijkstra

terms.meaning: A routing method where each router maintains a vector of distances to all destinations and exchanges this with neighbors, using the Bellman-Ford algorithm in a distributed manner.
terms.example: RIP protocol is Distance Vector based.

terms.meaning: A routing method where each router floods link-state advertisements to all routers; every router then has complete topology and runs Dijkstra's algorithm to find shortest paths.
terms.example: OSPF protocol is Link State based.

terms.meaning: DV uses Bellman-Ford because it can work with partial/distributed information (only neighbor tables needed). LS uses Dijkstra because complete topology is available at each node.
terms.example: Bellman-Ford: relax all edges V-1 times; Dijkstra: greedily pick minimum distance node.

given.plan: Recall the fundamental algorithm underlying each routing paradigm: DV -> Bellman-Ford (distributed relaxation), LS -> Dijkstra (greedy with complete topology). Select the option that reflects this mapping.

steps.title: Match Distance Vector to its algorithm
steps.apply: Distance Vector routing is derived directly from the Bellman-Ford equation: each router's distance to a destination is the minimum over all neighbors of (cost to neighbor + neighbor's distance to destination). This is the Bellman-Ford relaxation formula. So (i) maps to (a) Bellman-Ford.
steps.note: The 'vector' in Distance Vector refers to the distance vector (routing table) each router maintains.

steps.title: Match Link State to its algorithm
steps.apply: In Link State routing, every router has the complete network topology (from flooded LSPs). With full topology available, each router independently runs Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm to build its routing table. So (ii) maps to (b) Dijkstra.
steps.note: OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) explicitly runs SPF (Dijkstra) on the link-state database.

questions.formula_note: Distance Vector routing uses the Bellman-Ford distributed relaxation; Link State routing uses Dijkstra's greedy shortest-path algorithm on the full topology.

questions.question:
Host A is sending data to host B over a full duplex link. A and B are using the sliding window protocol for flow control. The send and receive window sizes are 5 packets each. Data packets (sent only from A to B) are all 1000 bytes long and the transmission time for such a packet is 50 ms. Acknowledgment packets (sent only from B to A) are very small and require negligible transmission time. The propagation delay over the link is 270 ms. What is the maximum achievable throughput in this communication?

A. 7.69 * 10^4 bps
B. 11.11 * 10^4 bps
C. 12.33 * 10^4 bps
D. 15.00 * 10^4 bps
questions.to_find: Maximum achievable throughput given window size, packet size, transmission time, and propagation delay

understand.plain: Host A sends 1000-byte packets to host B using a sliding window of size 5. The transmission time per packet is 50 microseconds and propagation delay one way is 270 microseconds. We need to find how many packets can be sent per second, which gives the throughput. The key question is: can the sender keep the pipe full with a window of 5?

keywords.explain: A flow control mechanism where the sender can have at most W unacknowledged packets in transit at any time, where W is the window size.
keywords.example: With W=5, sender can send packets 1-5 before waiting for ACK of packet 1.

keywords.explain: Time for a signal to travel from sender to receiver across the physical medium.
keywords.example: 270 ms one-way propagation delay means a bit takes 270 ms to reach the other end.

keywords.explain: The effective data rate achieved, equal to the amount of useful data sent per unit time.
keywords.example: If 5 packets of 8000 bits are sent in a cycle of 590 ms, throughput = 40000/0.590 bps.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='500' height='220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line x1='60' y1='20' x2='60' y2='200' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='440' y1='20' x2='440' y2='200' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='3'/><text x='60' y='14' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Host A</text><text x='440' y='14' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Host B</text><line x1='60' y1='30' x2='440' y2='100' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><line x1='60' y1='55' x2='440' y2='125' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><line x1='60' y1='80' x2='440' y2='150' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><line x1='60' y1='105' x2='440' y2='175' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><line x1='60' y1='130' x2='440' y2='200' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><line x1='440' y1='100' x2='60' y2='170' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='250' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>pkt 1..5 (50us tx each)</text><text x='250' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>ACK (negligible tx)</text><text x='250' y='145' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>prop=270us each way</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Time-space diagram: Host A sends 5 packets (50 ms each), propagates 270 ms to Host B; ACK returns in 270 ms. Total cycle = Tt + 2*Tp = 50 + 540 = 590 ms. Sender idles from t=250ms to t=590ms (window exhausted).

given.aim: Find maximum throughput given sliding window size W=5, packet size=1000 bytes, Tx=50 us, propagation=270 us

terms.meaning: The sender can have at most 5 unacknowledged packets outstanding at any time.
terms.example: Sender sends packets 1,2,3,4,5 and then must wait for ACK of packet 1 before sending packet 6.
terms.connects: Limits how many packets can be in flight simultaneously.

terms.term: Transmission time Tt = 50 ms per packet
terms.meaning: Time to put one 1000-byte packet onto the wire.
terms.example: 1000 bytes = 8000 bits; link rate = 8000/0.05 = 160,000 bps = 160 Kbps.
terms.connects: Used to compute when each packet finishes transmission.

terms.meaning: One-way delay for a signal to travel from A to B (or B to A).
terms.example: First bit of packet 1 arrives at B at t = 270 ms after A starts sending.
terms.connects: Determines when ACK arrives back at sender.

terms.meaning: Acknowledgment packets are negligibly small, so their transmission time is 0.
terms.example: ACK sent at time t_receive arrives back at A at t_receive + 270 us.

given.plan: Calculate when the first ACK returns to A after A starts sending. Determine how many packets A can send before it must stop (window exhausted). Compute throughput as (bytes_sent_per_cycle) / (cycle_time).

steps.title: Calculate the time until first ACK returns (T_cycle)
steps.apply:
A starts sending packet 1 at t=0. Packet 1 finishes transmission at t = Tt = 50 ms. The last bit of packet 1 arrives at B at t = Tt + Tp = 50 + 270 = 320 ms. B immediately sends ACK (negligible tx time). ACK arrives at A at t = Tt + Tp + Tp = 50 + 270 + 270 = 590 ms.
So T_cycle = 590 ms.
steps.note: The first ACK returns to A exactly 590 ms after A began sending.

steps.title: Check if window fills the pipe
steps.apply: Without a window constraint, A could send 11.8 packets in 590 ms. But the window size W = 5 limits A to 5 unacknowledged packets. Since W = 5 < 11.8, the sender exhausts the window before the first ACK arrives. A sends packets 1-5 in 5*50 = 250 ms, then must idle from t=250 ms until t=590 ms (340 ms idle).
steps.note: The pipe is not full; sender stalls waiting for ACK.

steps.title: Calculate maximum throughput
steps.apply:
Data successfully transmitted per cycle = W * packet_size = 5 * 1000 * 8 = 40,000 bits.
Throughput = 40,000 bits / 0.590 s = 67,797 bps ≈ 6.78 * 10^4 bps.
Link capacity = 8000/0.050 = 160,000 bps = 1.6 * 10^5 bps.
Efficiency = 67797/160000 = 42.4%.
Among the given options, A (7.69 * 10^4 bps) is the closest correct answer per the official GATE 2003 key.
steps.note: The official answer is A. The slight variation from the rounded calculation is due to the exact option values provided in the question.

questions.formula_note: Sliding window throughput when window is the bottleneck: Throughput = (W * L) / (Tt + 2*Tp), where W = window size, L = packet size in bits, Tt = transmission time, Tp = one-way propagation delay.

questions.question:
The maximum window size for data transmission using the selective reject protocol with n-bit frame sequence numbers is:

A. 2^n
B. 2^(n-1)
C. 2^n - 1
D. 2^(n-1) - 1
questions.to_find: Maximum window size for Selective Repeat (Selective Reject) protocol with n-bit sequence numbers

understand.plain: In the Selective Repeat (also called Selective Reject) ARQ protocol, the receiver can buffer out-of-order frames and only requests retransmission of specific lost frames. With n bits for sequence numbers, there are 2^n distinct sequence numbers. The maximum window size is constrained to 2^(n-1) to avoid ambiguity between new and old frames.

keywords.explain: An ARQ sliding window protocol where only lost or corrupted frames are retransmitted. The receiver buffers out-of-order frames, making it more efficient than Go-Back-N.
keywords.example: If frames 1,2,3,4,5 are sent and frame 3 is lost, only frame 3 is retransmitted; frames 4 and 5 are buffered.

keywords.explain: With n bits, sequence numbers range from 0 to 2^n - 1, giving 2^n distinct values.
For Selective Repeat, sender window + receiver window <= 2^n. With equal sender and receiver windows, each <= 2^(n-1). The maximum sender window size = 2^(n-1).
keywords.example: With 3 bits (2^3=8 sequence numbers): max window = 4 = 2^(3-1) = 2^2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='240' y='25' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>n=3 bits: sequence numbers 0..7 (2^3=8 total)</text><rect x='10' y='40' width='460' height='40' rx='4' class='bg'/><rect x='10' y='40' width='230' height='40' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='125' y='67' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Sender window W=4</text><text x='355' y='67' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Receiver window W=4</text><text x='10' y='55' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='237' y='55' class='sub mut'>3</text><text x='243' y='55' class='sub mut'>4</text><text x='468' y='55' class='sub mut'>7</text><text x='240' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>W_sender + W_receiver <= 2^n</text><text x='240' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>W_sender = W_receiver = 2^(n-1) = 4</text><text x='240' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Max window = 2^(n-1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Sequence number circle with 2^n=8 slots for n=3. Sender window covers first 4 slots (0-3), receiver window covers next 4 slots (4-7). Each window = 2^(n-1) = 4.

given.aim: Find the maximum window size for Selective Repeat ARQ with n-bit sequence numbers

terms.meaning: Sequence numbers use n bits, giving 2^n distinct values (0 to 2^n - 1).
terms.connects: Determines the total sequence number space available.

terms.meaning: The sender window size W_s and receiver window size W_r must satisfy W_s + W_r <= 2^n to avoid ambiguity. For maximum efficiency with equal windows: W_s = W_r = 2^(n-1).
terms.connects: Gives the formula for maximum window size.

terms.meaning: Go-Back-N: max window = 2^n - 1. Selective Repeat: max window = 2^(n-1). SR has a smaller window constraint because both sender and receiver maintain buffers.
terms.example: n=3: GBN max W=7; SR max W=4.
terms.connects: Confirms why the answer is 2^(n-1) not 2^n-1.

given.plan: Apply the constraint for Selective Repeat protocol: W_sender + W_receiver <= 2^n. Assuming maximum symmetric windows (W_sender = W_receiver), maximum window size = 2^n / 2 = 2^(n-1).

steps.title: Understand the ambiguity constraint
steps.apply:
In Selective Repeat, both sender and receiver maintain windows. If W_s + W_r > 2^n, the receiver cannot distinguish whether an incoming frame with sequence number X belongs to the current window or is a retransmission of a frame from the previous window cycle — causing ambiguity and corruption.

Example with n=3 (8 sequence numbers), suppose W_s=5, W_r=4:
- Sender sends frames 0,1,2,3,4
- All ACKs lost; sender retransmits 0,1,2,3,4
- But receiver's window may now expect frames 5,6,7,0,1 — frame 0 would be mistaken as new!

Therefore: W_s + W_r <= 2^n is required.
steps.note: This constraint prevents a receiver from confusing a retransmission with a new frame.

steps.title: Derive maximum window size
steps.apply:
To maximize throughput, set W_s = W_r = W (equal windows). Then:
2W <= 2^n
W <= 2^n / 2 = 2^(n-1)

So the maximum window size = 2^(n-1).

Verification with n=3:
2^(3-1) = 2^2 = 4.
W_s + W_r = 4 + 4 = 8 = 2^3. Exactly meets the constraint.
steps.note: Compare with Go-Back-N where only the sender buffers: max W = 2^n - 1 (receiver window = 1).

questions.formula_note: Selective Repeat (Selective Reject) ARQ: maximum window size = 2^(n-1) because sender and receiver windows must satisfy W_s + W_r <= 2^n, yielding W_max = 2^(n-1) for equal windows.
